display1.$description: The high display to deliver maximum visual impact as a graphic element in brand or marketing contexts.

when: hero or landing section on desktop where text functions as a visual graphic rather than readable prose
brand or campaign statement limited to a few words requiring the strongest typographic presence
avoid: mobile viewports → heading1 or heading2
text carrying meaningful or informational content → display2, display3, or display4

display2.$description: The mid display to deliver stronger visual impact than display3 in a page or hero context.

when: hero or landing headline requiring greater visual weight than display3 can provide
brand or campaign statement where maximum typographic presence is intentional
avoid: mobile viewports → heading1 or heading2
standard landing or hero headlines where display3 is sufficient → display3

display3.$description: The mid display to headline landing or hero sections as the default display scale on desktop.

when: primary headline of a landing page or hero section on desktop
brand or marketing statement requiring large visual weight with readable content
avoid: mobile viewports → heading1 or heading2
cases requiring stronger impact → display2

display4.$description: The low display to title subsections within a hero or marketing layout at the lowest display hierarchy.

when: subsection headline within a hero or landing area sitting below a display2 or display3 title
largest title in a promotional block that does not require full display1–3 scale
avoid: mobile viewports → heading1 or heading2

heading1.$description: The high heading to deliver the strongest visual impact as a hero headline on mobile.

when: primary hero headline on mobile where display tokens are not applicable
largest title in a mobile layout requiring maximum typographic presence
avoid: desktop hero headlines → display2, display3, or display4
desktop content section titles → heading2 or heading3

heading2.$description: The high heading to title a prominent content section or serve as a mobile hero headline at lower impact than heading1.

when: top-level title of a content section requiring strong visual emphasis, particularly in marketing contexts
mobile hero headline where heading1 provides more impact than needed
avoid: desktop hero headlines → display2, display3, or display4

heading3.$description: The mid heading to organize page-level content sections below the primary heading hierarchy.

when: section title that divides page-level content beneath heading1 or heading2
most prominent title within a content block that does not require heading1 or heading2 scale
avoid: top-level content section titles requiring strong emphasis → heading2
titles inside cards or panels → heading4

heading4.$description: The mid heading to establish the primary title within a card, panel, or contained component.

when: main title inside a card, panel, or other contained UI component
primary label of a grouped content block with defined boundaries
avoid: page-level section titles → heading3

heading5.$description: The low heading to title compact components or deliver strong emphasis in short label-like text.

when: primary title within a dialog, bottom sheet, or small-screen layout
short emphatic label requiring stronger visual weight than body or subtitle tokens
avoid: container titles where more hierarchy is needed → heading4
sub-titles or secondary labels within the same component → heading6

heading6.$description: The low heading to label secondary content or classify information alongside a heading.

when: sub-title or supporting label paired with heading3, heading4, or heading5
short classificatory label grouping or categorizing content such as counts or status indicators
avoid: primary titles within a component → heading5
labels inside badge or small contained components → subtitle2

subtitle1.$description: The high subtitle to deliver emphasis within body-scale text through increased font weight.

when: label or title requiring emphasis within a body text context
inline text that needs stronger visual weight than body2 without increasing font size
avoid: long body or paragraph text → body2
labels inside badge or small contained components → subtitle2

subtitle2.$description: The low subtitle to label minimal UI components or add emphasis at caption scale.

when: label inside a badge or similarly compact component requiring strong visual weight
caption-scale text requiring emphasis beyond body3
avoid: body or paragraph text → body2 or body3
labels outside compact components → subtitle1

body1.$description: The high body to provide supplementary description alongside large-scale headings.

when: descriptive text paired directly with a heading of 20px or larger as a supporting explanation
subtitle-like body text in hero or section contexts where heading scale demands larger supporting text
avoid: general body or paragraph text → body2
emphasized short labels → subtitle1

body2.$description: The high body to deliver readable content as the default body text scale.

when: general body or paragraph text in most UI contexts
long-form content requiring comfortable reading at the default text size
avoid: emphasized or bold inline text → subtitle1
supporting description for large headings → body1

body3.$description: The low body to provide descriptive captions for content elements at a reduced scale.

when: caption text describing an image, chart, or media element
supplementary description below a content block where body2 is too prominent
avoid: general body or paragraph text → body2
ultra-small labels where legibility is not a concern → body4

body4.$description: The low body to present the smallest readable text for space-constrained label contexts.

when: ultra-small label in a space-constrained UI element where no larger size fits
avoid: body or paragraph text where legibility matters → body2 or body3
captions or supplementary descriptions → body3

code1.$description: The first element in the code hierarchy. The default code text scale.

when: code block or inline code text
avoid: non-code text

code2.$description: The second and smallest element in the code hierarchy. The smallest code text scale.

when: code block or inline code text
avoid: non-code text
